coredata.dc:title: A non-oxidation reduction strategy for highly selective detection of ascorbic acid by “on-off-on” photoluminescence switches
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Wei
Lu, Yuexiang
Li, Jiaqing
Zhang, Xiwen
Wang, Nan
Liu, Yueying
coredata.dc:description: Great progress was made to detect ascorbic acid (AA) based on the photoluminescent nanoprobes/oxidant complexes by using the reducibility of AA. However, some reducing substances including cysteine and glutathione disturbed the accuracy of AA assay. Herein, a non-oxidation reduction regulation strategy was fabricated to construct a novel “on-off-on” photoluminescence model for highly selective determination of AA. Initially, copper nanoclusters capsulated into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (CuNCs@ZIF-8) was achieved. And then inorganic phosphate ions quenched the emission of CuNCs@ZIF-8 owing to the decomposition of ZIF-8 shell, rendering the signal in “turn-off” state. The linear range for phosphate ions was 1.0–60 μM with LOD of 0.35 μM. Interestingly, upon introducing AA into CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate, the emission of CuNCs@ZIF-8 recovered possibly due to the electronegativity improvement of metal core and surface rigidified through hydrogen bond interaction, and the system signal was “turned-on”. The LOD of AA was as low as 51 nM in a good linear range from 0.1 to 40 μM. Furthermore, CuNCs@ZIF-8 were used for AA detecting in tablets, beverages, and fresh fruits with the satisfactory recoveries from 96% to 104%. More importantly, a paper-based analytical device containing CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate were evaluated for the visual sensing of AA in food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8
Photoluminescent analysis
Ascorbic acid
Inorganic phosphate ions
Paper-based device

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assay of AA in vitamin C tablets, beverages, and fresh fruits
“Turn-off” PL mode of CuNCs@ZIF-8 for inorganic phosphate assay
PL enhancement and high stability of CuNCs@ZIF-8
Sensing mechanism for the detection of inorganic phosphate
Sensitive and selective detection of inorganic phosphate
“Turn-on” PL mode of CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − for the determination of AA
“Turn-on” PL mode for AA sensing mechanism
Sensitive and selective determination of AA
Detection of AA in vitamin C tablets, beverages, and fresh fruits

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM images of CuNCs. (B) TEM of CuNCs/ZIF-8. (C) elemental mapping of CuNCs@ZIF-8 (Zn, Cu, S, and N). (D) Particle size distribution of CuNCs, CuNCs@ZIF-8 determined by DLS. (E) XRD pattern of ZIF-8 (black line) and CuNCs@ZIF-8 (red line), and CuNCs/ZIF-8 (red line). (F) FTIR spectra of GSH, CuNCs, ZIF-8 and CuNCs@ZIF-8.

(A) PL spectra of CuNCs, magnified CuNCs by 30 times, CuNCs + Zn2+, and CuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. Insets: photographs after excited with a 365 nm UV lamp. (B) Time-resolved fluorescence lifetime analysis of CuNCs and CuNCs@ZIF-8. (C) Relative PL intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8 in 30 days. (D) PL spectra of CuNCs@ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of Pi. (E) TEM images of CuNCs@ZIF-8+Pi. (F) Particle size distribution of CuNCs@ZIF-8 before and after Pi by DLS.

CuNCs@ZIF-8/PB in the absence and presence of AA: (A) PL excitation and emission spectra. (B) Absorption spectra. (C) TEM images of CuNCs@ZIF-8/PB with AA. (D) Particle size distribution by DLS. (E) Time-resolved fluorescence lifetime analysis. (F) PL emission spectra of CuNCs, enlarged 30 times of CuNCs, and CuNCs + Zn2+ with and without AA.

(A) Effect of kinds of inorganic phosphates (PO4 3− > HPO4 2− > H2PO4 −) on the PL intensities of CuNCs@ZIF-8 before and after adding inorganic phosphates. Inset: corresponding photographs under a 365 nm UV lamp. (B) Photographs of paper-based three sensing system (CuNCs@ZIF-8 + PO4 3−, CuNCs@ZIF-8 + HPO4 2−, and CuNCs@ZIF-8 + H2PO4 −) before and after addition of AA. (C) PL quenching intensity versus the various concentration of H2PO4 −. (D) Incubation time versus PL intensity change of CuNCs@ZIF-8 + H2PO4 − before and after the addition of AA.

PL emission spectra of the different systems without H2PO4 − after adding various concentrations of AA: (A) CuNCs@ZIF-8. (B) CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 −. (C) Their corresponding plot of I/I0 vs concentration of AA in the range from 0 to 200 μM. (D) Linear fitting with AA concentration ranging from 1 to 80 μM for CuNCs@ZIF-8 and 0.1 to 40 μM for CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − . (E) Photographs of paper-based two sensing systems including CuNCs@ZIF-8 and CuNCs@ZIF-8 + H2PO4 − after addition of AA (0, 1, 10, and 100 μM). (F) PL intensities of CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − after adding different anions (50 μM).
Schematic diagram of AA detection using CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 −
Determination of AA in three types of tablets, four types of beverages and two types of fresh fruits.

head.ce:title: A non-oxidation reduction strategy for highly selective detection of ascorbic acid by “on-off-on” photoluminescence switches

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing, 100048, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing, 100048, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Nuclear Energy Technology, Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Nuclear Energy Technology
Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Nuclear Energy Technology, Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Great progress was made to detect ascorbic acid (AA) based on the photoluminescent nanoprobes/oxidant complexes by using the reducibility of AA. However, some reducing substances including cysteine and glutathione disturbed the accuracy of AA assay. Herein, a non-oxidation reduction regulation strategy was fabricated to construct a novel “on-off-on” photoluminescence model for highly selective determination of AA. Initially, copper nanoclusters capsulated into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (CuNCs@ZIF-8) was achieved. And then inorganic phosphate ions quenched the emission of CuNCs@ZIF-8 owing to the decomposition of ZIF-8 shell, rendering the signal in “turn-off” state. The linear range for phosphate ions was 1.0–60 μM with LOD of 0.35 μM. Interestingly, upon introducing AA into CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate, the emission of CuNCs@ZIF-8 recovered possibly due to the electronegativity improvement of metal core and surface rigidified through hydrogen bond interaction, and the system signal was “turned-on”. The LOD of AA was as low as 51 nM in a good linear range from 0.1 to 40 μM. Furthermore, CuNCs@ZIF-8 were used for AA detecting in tablets, beverages, and fresh fruits with the satisfactory recoveries from 96% to 104%. More importantly, a paper-based analytical device containing CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate were evaluated for the visual sensing of AA in food safety.

A unique “on-off-on” photoluminescence assay for the highly selective determination of AA has developed by using a non-oxidation reduction mechanism based on copper nanoclusters encapsulated in zeolitic imidazolate framework-8.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid, also called AA or vitamin C, is one kind of water dissolvable vitamin, which is mostly found in fresh fruits and vegetables. Its content is regarded as a vital index of the freshness of fruit and its nutrition [1]. However, AA as an indispensable nutrient cannot be synthesized directly by the body. So, dietary intake of AA in fresh vegetables and fruits, beverages, vitamin C tablets is an easy way to supply AA for keeping up human health. AA as a basic supplement plays a pivotal role in body growth, metabolism and development process due to acting as a potent reducing and excellent antioxidant properties [2]. The concentration of AA in healthy human plasma is determined to be ranging from 35 to 85 μM [3]. The insufficient AA admissions is profoundly related to genuine illnesses counting scurvy, cancer, and Parkinson's malady. While excessive AA intake can cause kidney stones and stomach cramps [4]. The recent research demonstrates that AA might prevent and treat infection against COVID-19 [5]. Therefore, it is urgent to analyze AA concentration in food and clinical fields, which is conducive to diet guidance and quality monitoring of medicine, beverages, and fresh fruits.

Numerous strategies have been created for AA analysis, counting high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with electrochemical detector [6], iodometry [7], titration analysis [8], electrochemistry [9], colorimetry [10–12], and fluorescence analysis [13]. Among them, fluorescence strategies with the advantages of rapid analysis, high sensitivity, less sample consumption, and easy operations have sparked tremendous interest. So far, various nanomaterials or fluorescent organic dyes have been utilized for sensing AA precisely, including organic dyes [14], carbon materials [1], semiconductor quantum dots, upconversion nanoparticles, metal organic framework [15], and metal nanoclusters [16,17]. In order to achieve higher analytical sensitivity and overcome the false positive results, incorporation luminescent probe with oxidants as quenchers including MnO4 − [18], MnO2 [19], ClO2 − [2], H2O2 [20], and Cr2O7 2− [21] in the presence of AA might create novel “on-off-on” switchable sensors, in which the initial photoluminescence is firstly quenched by quenchers (from “on” to “off”) and then recovered by AA (from off to on) due to the response of analytes towards quenchers [22]. Some metal ions also acted as quenchers in the “on-off-on” sensors, such as Hg2+ [23], Cu2+ [24], Fe3+ [1,25–27]. However, most fluorophores and nanomaterials show aggregation-caused quenching effect, which leads to poor sensitivity and stability. It has been reported that encapsulating dyes or nanomaterials into metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) can avoid aggregation and improve their fluorescence performance. Zeolite imidazole skeleton (ZIFs) is a subclass of MOFs composed of metal ions and imidazole joints, which have attracted lots of attention due to their simple synthesis and excellent performance. For example, the gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) encapsulated into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) coupling MnO4 − were designed for the detection of AA assay by means of redox reaction [18]. Rhodamine B embedded into MOF nanocomposite was synthesized for the detection of AA by Fe3+ as an oxidant based “on-off-on” fluorescent model. Obviously, the majority of the above nanosensors are only based on the reducibility of AA to recover their fluorescent performance. However, a few reducing components in serum such as cysteine and glutathione (GSH) may interfere with the analysis results of AA. To address this issue, an organic dye was used as a fluorescence probe via a non-oxidation reduction method for the highly selective detection of AA [28]. As far as we know, there is no report on the determination of AA by using other nanomaterials as a luminescent probe based on the non-oxidation reduction strategy.

Metal nanoclusters, as a new type of photoluminescent material, have attracted more and more research interests including environmental monitoring, food safety and disease diagnosis [29–31]. The unique properties of nanoclusters as ultra-small size, long lifetime, large Stokes shift, and great biocompatibility make them ideal for the construction of nanosensors. Recently, intensive studies were focused on the utilization of gold nanoclusters/oxidant complex for the detection of AA through oxidation reduction strategy [17,32]. Compared with gold nanoclusters, copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) have pulled in increasingly consideration due to their advantages of easy availability, abundant resource, and relatively low cost [33–35]. In this work, a new non-oxidation reduction regulation strategy based on on-off-on photoluminescence signal was constructed to detect AA by using CuNCs encapsulated into ZIF-8. As exhibited in Scheme 1 , glutathione-templated CuNCs were firstly prepared directly by adjusting pH ≈ 7.0 with NaOH. And then CuNCs and Zn2+ was mixed to form a stable combination due to the aggregation induction effect (AIE). Finally, upon introducing 2-methylimidazole, the in-situ encapsulation of CuNCs into the porous microcrystalline ZIF-8 was successfully created by Zn2+ induced ZIF-8 growth strategy. Notably, the PL emission and stability was dramatically improved compared with free CuNCs. Inorganic phosphate ions can destroy the structure of ZIF-8 shell because of the coordination between Zn2+ and phosphate, and then CuNCs were released, which decreased PL intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8. However, after AA was introduced to CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate solution, the PL emission of the mixture restored. This luminescence recovery could be attributed to the improved electronegativity of metal core via ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) and surface rigidified by hydrogen bond interaction between GSH and AA. The established sensor platform was then successfully employed for the determination of AA in vitamin C tablets, beverages, and fresh fruits. In particular, paper strips were prepared for the visible detection of AA.

Copper nitrate, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM) and l-glutathione reducing agent were purchased from Maclean (Shanghai, China). Sodium phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, and sodium dihydrogen phosphate were gotten from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Sodium hydroxide, Glucose (Glu), Maltose (Malt), and Fructose (Fru) were obtained from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). Ascorbic acid (AA), Arginine (Arg), Lysine (Lys), and cysteine (Cys) were acquired from Beijing BioDee Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). All other reagents were at least analytical grade. The ultrapure water was produced by Milli-Q purification system.

The photoluminescence and UV–vis spectra were gotten by a Molecular Devices SpectraMax M2 Multi-Mode Microplate Reader. The D8 Advance diffractometer was used to obtain the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were performed on a Nicolet 380 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were acquired on a Hitachi (H-7650, 80 kV) transmission electron microscope. The hydrodynamic size and Zeta potential of the sample were measured using Malvern Zetasizer (U.K.).

The CuNCs was synthesized according to previous study with some modifications [36]. Firstly, copper nitrate hexahydrate (5 mL, 10 mM) was added to glutathione (5 mL, 40 mM) and kept stirring for about 5 min until getting the pale yellow CuNCs. After the pH of the mixture was balanced to around 7.0 with NaOH (1.0 M), the colorless and transparent CuNCs was obtained.

To synthesize CuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites, Zn(NO3)2 (1 mL, 100 mM) was added to the synthesized GSH-CuNCs (2 mL) beneath stirring for about 5 min at room temperature. Subsequently, 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM) (1 mL, 365 mM) solution was gradually added to the above mixture. After reacting for around 10 min, the resultant CuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for approximately 5 min. And the ultimate product was washed and centrifuged three times using methanol and ultrapure water in arrange, and after that dried in a vacuum at 60 °C.

For the detection of phosphate ions, 40 μL of phosphate ions solutions with different concentrations were added into 200 μL of CuNCs@ZIF-8 (2.0 mg/mL). Then, add 60 μL ultrapure water to adjust the total volume of this system to 300 μL. The mix solution was incubated for 25 min at room temperature. Then, 200 μL of the reaction solution was added into 96-well plate to recorded the PL spectra under excitation at 340 and emission wavelength of 600 nm in a microplate reader.

The mixture of H2PO4 − ions (70 μM), CuNCs@ZIF-8 (1.3 mg/mL), and AA with different concentrations (0–200 μM) was incubated for 25 min at normal temperature. Then, 200 μL of the reaction solution was added into 96-well plate and the PL spectra were recorded in a microplate reader. Triplicate tests were run on each PL measurement.

To evaluate the selectivity of the above system for detection of AA, metal ions (Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, and Al3+), anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, NO2 −, CO3 2−, and Ac−), amino acid (Arg, Lys, Cys, and glutamic acid (Glu)], carbohydrates (Malt, and Fru), and glutathione (GSH) as interference were determined. The concentration of each interfering substance is the same as AA at 50 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of AA in vitamin C tablets, beverages, and fresh fruits
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of sensing with CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − in real applications, three kinds of vitamin C tablets, four types of different beverages (vitamin, orange, grape, and tomato) and two kinds of fresh fruits with various AA levels were purchased from the local supermarket and selected as the representative samples to evaluate the analytical performance of the proposed method. The extraction of vitamin C from each tablet sample was carried out base on the previous study [37]. Vitamin C tablets were crushed into powder with a mortar, and dissolved with ultrapure water. To separate the supernatant, the mixture was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. Two kinds of fresh fruits were treated as follows. Firstly, it was necessary to peel samples of grapes and cherry tomatoes and use a specialized grinder to grind the edible portions into homogenate. The obtained homogenate was then centrifuged at 6000 rmp for 10 min. As for extract solution from three kinds of tablets, the mixture solution from two kinds of fresh fruits, and four types of beverages were taken individually and filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter to get the filtrates. Additionally, the standard addition recovery test was used for AA detection in these real samples. Statistical analyses were applied to the collected testing data (n = 3).

The GSH-CuNCs were prepared by using GSH as both reducing agent and protecting agent by adjusting pH ≈ 7.0 at room temperature. As displayed in Fig. 1 A, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images revealed that CuNCs were spherical with an average diameter of 2.6 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which was agreed with the pre-existing literature [36]. The ZIF-8 shell was in-situ shaped on the surface of CuNCs. Fig. 1B and Fig. S1 showed that the size of CuNCs@ZIF-8 was approximately 255.6 nm. Both ZIF-8 and CuNCs@ZIF-8 photoluminescent probes exhibit regular rhomboid dodecahedral structures. The energy-dispersive spectroscopic (EDS) mapping imaging of Zn, Cu, N, and S elements of CuNCs@ZIF-8 provided additional evidence that CuNCs incorporate into ZIF-8 (Fig. 1C). The hydrodynamic diameter of CuNCs, and CuNCs@ZIF-8 was about 5.4 nm and 255.6 nm by dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis, respectively (Fig. 1D). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was implemented on pure ZIF-8 and CuNCs@ZIF-8. As illustrated in Fig. 1E, all the main XRD peaks of CuNCs@ZIF-8 were consistent with the dodecahedron of ZIF-8 nanocrystalline, which shows that the crystal integrity of ZIF-8 is not affected by the encapsulation of CuNCs.

At the same time, FTIR test results also verified that CuNCs packaging did not affect the structure of ZIF-8 (Fig. 1F). The Cu–S bond was formed at the metal surface as evidenced by the disappearance of the characteristic S–H stretching vibration in CuNCs and CuNCs@ZIF-8 at a wavelength of 2525 cm−1. The absorption peaks in the range of 3200–3400 cm−1 belong to the tensile vibration of O–H and N–H bands and the absorption peaks at 1310 cm−1 belong to C–N stretching vibration. Furthermore, the bands identified by the spectra (421 cm−1) as belonging to the Zn–N stretching mode. Besides, the element compositions of CuNCs and valence states of Cu element was confirmed by XPS. The four primary peaks at 285.0, 532.0, 400.0, and 163.0 eV corresponded to the XPS characteristic peaks for C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p (Fig. S2A). Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 of Cu(0) and Cu(I), respectively, were attributed to two separate peaks at 932.1 and 952.2 eV (Fig. S2B). The presence of Cu(II) was excluded by the absence of the typical peak at 942.0 eV [36]. These results showed that the CuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Turn-off” PL mode of CuNCs@ZIF-8 for inorganic phosphate assay
PL enhancement and high stability of CuNCs@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 A and Fig. S3, upon excitation at 340 nm, CuNCs displayed an emission band with a central wavelength of 620 nm. After the addition of Zn2+ at 33.3 mM, the emission was amplified up to over 100-fold and blue-shifted to 600 nm (red curve of Fig. 2A). Under a 365 nm UV lamp, the solution changes from dark to orange in color (insert of Fig. 2A). The PL enhancement probably is attributed to the aggregation induced emission (AIE) behavior [36]. Interestingly, the emission of CuNCs@ZIF-8 was increased up to about 120-fold and blue-shifted to 590 nm compared to CuNCs. The color of corresponding solution became more intense orange. The average PL lifetimes of the CuNCs and CuNCs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 2B) were 3.95 μs and 15.68 μs, respectively. The PL emission mechanism was assigned to LMCT or LMMCT transitions via a metal-centered triplet state, which could explain the appearance of phosphorescence with an emission redshift and long μs lifetimes [38]. The PL enhancement might be ascribed to mainly two reasons. The carboxyl groups on the surface of CuNCs were coordinated with Zn2+, which exhibited aggregation induced emission (AIE) behavior (Fig. S4) to reduce the vibration and rotation of ligands. On the other hand, the ZIF-8 microporous structure restricted the free movement of CuNCs. These factors inhibited the non-radiation pathway of the energized electrons reverting to their ground state and increased radiation transition. The intensity of CuNCs decreased by 80% after 7 h (Fig. S5). Notably, it was found that the PL intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8 only decreased by 20% within 30 days (Fig. 2C), indicating its good stability. This improved stability was credited to ZIF shell's defense against oxidizing species in the air. In addition, CuNCs@ZIF-8 exhibits the excellent stability under daylight/UV–vis radiation and the high ionic strength (0.4 M, NaCl) (Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism for the detection of inorganic phosphate
ce:section.ce:para: The PL intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8 was efficiently decreased when phosphate ions exist (Fig. 2D). TEM images demonstrated that ZIF-8 shell was damaged (Fig. 2E). Simultaneously, the hydrodynamic size measured by DLS showed a particle size in the range from 255.6 down to 105.3 nm, indicating the decomposition of CuNCs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 2F). CuNCs migrated from ZIF-8, resulting in photoluminescence quenching. It has been demonstrated that ZIF-8 shell can be decomposed through phosphate ions coordination with Zn2+ [39]. Therefore, CuNCs@ZIF-8 was developed for the measurement of Pi based on “on-off” photoluminescent model in the environment and body fluids. Inorganic phosphate (Pi) as an important component played vital roles in many biological processes of human body [40], but also acted as a crucial role in maintaining acid-base balance and electrolyte equilibrium [41]. Numerous illnesses, including osteomalacia, renal failure, and hyperparathyroidism, were linked to the imbalance of Pi [42]. In addition, Pi also played a decisive role in the balanced development of ecological environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of inorganic phosphate
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions involving reaction time and temperature were modified to provide greater sensing performance of phosphate ions. The emission of CuNCs@ZIF-8 was rapidly quenched after introducing phosphate ions, and stabilized progressively within 25 min (Fig. S7A). So, reaction time was set to 25 min. In addition, the luminescence quenching efficiency increased with the rise of the temperature (Fig. S7B). When the temperature exceeds 30 °C, the strengthening trend slows down, so 30 °C was selected in following experiments. Investigating CuNCs@ZIF-8's responses to various phosphate ions were done under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. S8A and Fig. S8B, the PL intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8 at 600 nm decreased step by step as the concentration of phosphate ions increased to be 200 μM. When the concentration was in the range of 0.6–60 μM, the relationship between intensity and phosphate ions concentrations possessed a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.991) in Fig. S8C. The limit detection of CuNCs@ZIF-8 for phosphate ions was 0.35 μM according to the calculation of 3 σ/k, where σ was the standard deviation of the blank and k was the slope of the linear fitting. The normal concentration of urinary phosphate ions ranged from 16 to 42 mmol/day [40]. So, CuNCs @ZIF-8 could be credible for detecting phosphate ions in urine. Furthermore, to ensure the selectivity of this strategy for phosphate ions analysis, the influence of the potential interfering ions (ATP, PPi, F−, Cl−, Br−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, NO2 −, CO3 2−, and Ac−) were systematically investigated. The concentration of each interfering substance was the same as phosphate ions at 60 μM. According to Fig. S8D, we can see other substances showed little influence on the PL intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8 while phosphate ions can effectively reduce the response of this system, indicating the outstanding selectivity toward phosphate ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Turn-on” PL mode of CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − for the determination of AA
“Turn-on” PL mode for AA sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The PL intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate was increased by 100%, without changing the peak position after the introduction of AA in Fig. 3 A. Thus, a “Turn-on” PL mode was achieved. The UV–vis absorption did not change in a noticeable way compared with pure CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate (Fig. 3B). As TEM image (Fig. 3C) shows, the rough and irregular edges of CuNCs@ZIF-8/Pi were etched after introducing AA, leading to that the morphology of nanomaterial is close to spherical. Simultaneously, the hydrodynamic average size was found to decrease slightly when CuNCs@ZIF-8/Pi was exposed to AA (Fig. 3D). The PL decay curves of CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate were presented before and after adding AA. As shown in Fig. 3E, an average PL lifetime of 14.31 μs for CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate were obtained, which were almost the same as for the AA-treated CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate with average lifetime of 13.86 μs. The formation of new species as a result of AA treatment can be ruled out by comparison of PL emission spectra of CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate and AA-treated ones, which confirms the same nature of the emitting species in both cases.

To further verify the PL emission enhancement or CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate after introducing AA, the PL emission of CuNCs, CuNCs + Zn2+, and CuNCs@ZIF-8 were performed in the presence of AA (Fig. 3F and Fig. S9A). After being treated with AA for only CuNCs@ZIF-8, the shape of nanocomposites has changed from dodecahedron into approximate sphere (Figs. S9B and S9C) accompanying with moderate PL intensity increase. This data indicates the corner and edge regions of CuNCs@ZIF-8 shell was decomposed upon adding AA possibly due to the weak acidic of AA with its pKa = 4.25. The PL decay curves of CuNCs@ZIF-8 without and with AA are shown in Fig. S10, where no significant change in the averaged lifetime was observed, approving the absence of new substances. Similarly, after the treatment of AA, the PL response of CuNCs and CuNCs + Zn2+ obviously increases by 35.3%, and 45.1% (Fig. 3F and Fig. S11), respectively. Based on the above results, we speculate that the PL enhancement of CuNCs, CuNCs + Zn2+, CuNCs@ZIF-8, and CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate were possibly assigned to electron-rich groups from AA and rigidified GSH ligand surface from hydrogen bond between GSH and AA. This result is consistent with the previous report, where PL emission of CuNCs can be boosted by host-guest interactions between the electron-rich chemical groups in GSH and AA-protected CuNCs [43,44].

In order to obtain optimum conditions for AA detection, several tests such as the types of phosphate ions, concentrations of quencher phosphate ions, and the incubation time were carried out. As seen from Fig. 4 A, three kinds of inorganic phosphates (PO4 3−, HPO4 2−, and H2PO4 −) could all trigger PL quenching effect of CuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. The order of PL intensity change was CuNCs@ZIF-8/PO4 3− > CuNCs@ZIF-8/HPO4 2− > CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 −, suggesting that the order of their high binding ability to Zn2+ ions was PO4 3− > HPO4 2− > H2PO4 −. And then the intensity of their sensing system was dramatically increased after the addition of AA. The system changed from dark to orange in color when excited by a UV lamp at 365 nm (insert in Fig. 4A). Due to the necessity of on-site testing, a portable test paper for AA detection was made by using three kinds of sensing systems. Obviously, the PL recovery of CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − was easier to be observed by naked eyes compared with CuNCs@ZIF-8/HPO4 2− and CuNCs@ZIF-8/PO4 3− (Fig. 4B). Therefore, H2PO4 − ions were selected as the PL quencher. After the addition of H2PO4 −, the CuNCs@ZIF-8 system exhibits weak acid (pH ≈ 6.0). To demonstrate the degradation of ZIF-8 by H2PO4 − is not related to the acid instability of ZIF-8, the pH value adjusted by using HCl was the same as when H2PO4 − was present, the PL intensity and morphology of CuNCs@ZIF-8 were not obviously changed (Fig. S12), indicating the acid-induced degradation of PL probe unlikely occurred. For accurate AA detection, the impact of H2PO4 − concentration on PL quenching was examined. As described in Fig. 4C, the PL quenching efficiency (1-I/I0, where I and I0 represented the PL intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8 at 600 nm in the presence and absence of H2PO4 −) increased gradually with the increase of H2PO4 −. The maximum quenching efficiency of 68.3% was observed at H2PO4 − concentration of 70 μM. The deficiency or excessive H2PO4 − was not helpful in sensitive detection of AA. Therefore, the concentration of H2PO4 − at 70 μM was chosen for sensing AA. As seen in Fig. 4D, as soon as AA was added to the system of CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 −, the PL intensity of ΔI (ΔII0–I, where I and I0 represented the PL intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − in the presence and absence of AA) reached a stable state in 25 min. Therefore, the incubation time of 25 min was chosen for the detection of AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective determination of AA
ce:section.ce:para: CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − was added with various concentrations of AA under optimal conditions, and changes in PL intensity were investigated. A comparison test was also conducted on the evolution of the PL spectra of CuNCs@ZIF-8. As displayed in Fig. 5 A and B, the PL intensities of CuNCs@ZIF-8 and CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − at 600 nm were enhanced after the addition of AA in the range of 0–200 μM. The PL ratios of I/I0 (where I and I0 represented the PL intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8/CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − in the presence and absence of AA) increased gradually with the increase of AA in Fig. 5C. As described Fig. 5D, the linear regression equation for CuNCs@ZIF-8 was 2.5 × 10−3 CAA + 1.04 in the concentration range from 1.0 to 80 μM with LOD of 440 nM. For CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − system, the linear regression equation was 2.1 × 10−2 CAA + 1.01 (0.1–40 μM) and LOD was estimated to be 51 nM. The linear relationships demonstrated that CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − was discovered to show greater sensitivity than CuNCs@ZIF-8 for detecting AA. Compared with most reported AA sensors (Table S1), CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − as a PL emission probe displayed a better performance. Further, the possibility of using filter paper by dropwise addition CuNCs@ZIF-8 or CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − for final evaluation of the non-instrumental AA determination under a 365 nm UV lamp, there was no obvious color difference between the filter paper with CuNCs@ZIF-8 after incubation of different concentrations of AA (0, 1, 10, and 100 μM) in the upper row of Fig. 5E. While the orange degree of the test paper with CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − increased significantly with an increase in AA concentrations in the bottom row of Fig. 5E. The color change of the test paper was visible to the naked eye. Therefore, the CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − system was then used in the visual detection of AA.

To further evaluate the selectivity of CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − for AA analysis, the influence of metal ions, amino acids, carbohydrates, and other potentially co-existed substances were chosen to examine the selectivity of this method. As displayed in Fig. 5F and Fig. S13, AA shows a stronger PL recovery ability on CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − than any other interference. Almost all interfering substances, even GSH, a biological reducing substance, had no obvious influence on the PL intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 −, suggesting that this method exhibited the highly selective detection of AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in vitamin C tablets, beverages, and fresh fruits
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practical application of CuNCs@ZIF-8/H2PO4 − system for real sample analysis, the proposed approach was used to identify AA in several vitamin C tablets, beverages, and fresh fruits. As depicted in Table 1 , the tested results for three different commercial brands of vitamin C tablets were well consistent with their composition descriptions. Under the same detection conditions, AA levels in four kinds of beverages and two types of fresh fruits were successfully measured in Fig. S14. The content of AA is the highest in tomato beverage. For fresh fruits, the concentration of AA in cherry tomatoes is higher than that in grapes. To further ensure the reliability and accuracy of this sensing system, the standard addition recovery test was carried out. As can be seen from Table 1, the recovery rate was between 96% and 104%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) was less than 4%, demonstrating a high practicability and reliability of this system for monitoring AA in food samples. Further, the proposed sensing system was employed to measure AA in human serum samples. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries were ranged from 97.63% to 102.52%, suggesting the good accuracy of the proposed approach.

In summary, a strategy of the non-oxidation reduction reaction was fabricated to build an innovative “on-off-on” photoluminescence model for detecting AA with high selectivity based on CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate. Importantly, the photoluminescence performance and stability of CuNCs embedded into ZIF-8 were greatly improved owing to AIE behavior and the protective effect of ZIF shell against oxidizing species in air. The shell of ZIF could be decomposed due to the coordination of Zn2+ and phosphate ions, which made CuNCs dispersion and accompanying luminescence quenching effect. CuNCs@ZIF-8 could realize the selective detection of phosphate ions based on “turn-off” mode. After adding AA to CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate, the photoluminescence emission of CuNCs@ZIF-8 restored, which was ascribed to the characteristic of AA with electron-rich groups and rigidified ligand surface through hydrogen bonding interaction between GSH and AA. The “turn-on” switchable sensing mechanism was constructed for the detection of AA. More importantly, the luminescent probe CuNCs@ZIF-8/phosphate could efficiently detect AA in vitamin C tablets, beverages, and fresh fruits. Particularly, a test paper was developed to realize the visual detection of AA. Therefore, this sensing assay has potential to detect AA in the field of the food safety.

Wei Huang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Yuexiang Lu: Conceptualization, Review, Project administration. Jiaqing Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiwen Zhang: Investigation, Validation. Nan Wang: Methodology, Software. Yueying Liu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21775087) and Scientific Research Project of Beijing Educational Committee (Grant No. KM202010028007).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2023.111419.
